title: podman, signals,
body:
This isn't a bug report; it's a pathetic plea for help. A desperate cry into the ether, hoping it will trigger an Aha! or a Hmmm from someone.

TL;DR podman containers sometimes aren't acknowledging or propagating signals.

Run this in one shell:

    # podman run --attach=stdout --sig-proxy=true --tty=false  registry.access.redhat.com/rhel7/rhel:latest bash -c 'for NUM in `seq 1 64`; do trap "echo Received $NUM, ignoring..." $NUM; done; echo READY; while :; do sleep 0.1; done'
    READY

Then in another:

    # CID=$(podman ps |tail -1|cut -c1-4)                  ! I know, I know
    # PID=$(podman inspect --format '{{.State.Pid}}' $CID)
    # kill -22 $PID

This produces the expected output back in terminal one:

    Received 22, ignoring...

Same with 3, 2, 23, 24, 10. This is exactly as expected. It's good.

When the same `podman run` command is run within docker-autotest (super complicated pythony python framework), autotest never sees the `Received XX, ignoring` output. It does see `READY`. 

According to strace, podman under docker-autotest is receiving the signal:

    epoll_wait(4,

then, on signal:

    epoll_wait(4, 0x7fff1d46a060, 128, -1)  = -1 EINTR (Interrupted system call)
    --- SIGUSR1 {si_signo=SIGUSR1, si_code=SI_USER, si_pid=1302, si_uid=0} ---
    rt_sigreturn({mask=[]})                 = -1 EINTR (Interrupted system call)
    epoll_wait(4,

I've also tried `grep Sig /proc/$PID/status` and see nothing suspicious.

It doesn't seem to be tty-related: I've tried (command-line) `</dev/null` or `|cat` or `>/tmp/mylog`. Same successful behavior. It doesn't seem to be a buffering issue: `podman logs $CID` shows the Received lines when run from terminal, and does not when run from docker-autotest (i.e. same behavior under docker-autotest).

The final bit of weirdness: when running under docker-autotest, the first `podman inspect` returns the pid of the `podman` command. This seems normal and expected. But when I'm poking around in another term, while docker-autotest is running and waiting (in vain) for output, the same `podman inspect` returns a different PID - the bash command started above, but now a child of `conmon`.
```
root      8722  0.0  0.0  86080  2208 ?        Ssl  22:41   0:00 /usr/libexec/crio/conmon -c 867dbb8f2b6f5f0a880f729f3247f40b7b2300fa102f0e2ae242cca567945695 -u 867dbb8f2b6f5f0a880f729f3247f40b7b2300fa102f0e2ae242cca567945695 -r /usr/bin/runc -b /var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/867dbb8f2b6f5f0a880f729f3247f40b7b2300fa102f0e2ae242cca567945695/userdata -p /var/run/containers/storage/overlay-containers/867dbb8f2b6f5f0a880f729f3247f40b7b2300fa102f0e2ae242cca567945695/userdata/pidfile -l /var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/867dbb8f2b6f5f0a880f729f3247f40b7b2300fa102f0e2ae242cca567945695/userdata/ctr.log --exit-dir /var/run/libpod/exits --socket-dir-path /var/run/libpod/socket -t
root      8738  0.2  0.0  11784  2660 pts/0    Ss+  22:41   0:00  \_ bash -c for NUM in `seq 1 64`; do trap "echo Received $NUM, ignoring..." $NUM; done; echo READY; while :; do sleep 0.1; done
root     12156  0.0  0.0   4328   628 pts/0    S+   22:46   0:00      \_ sleep 0.1
```
Sending a signal to that process (`kill -10 8738`) causes docker-autotest to see the expected `Received` line and move on to the next test.

I'm sorry for the poor quality of this report. I'm just really lost.

comments.body:
We don't have `--sig-proxy` implemented yet. It should probably pop up a warning or something to that effect to prevent confusion.

Or finish implementing signal handling... I might take a crack at it if I can find the time.

So this is fully expected, because our signal handling doesn't work, podman itself eats the signals and the container doesn't get them
The inspect issue is strange, though. I would think we should report the container PID always? It also should never change once first set, I think, though I'm less sure of that
I also suspect --attach {stdin, stdout, stderr} are all nonfunctional. We presently don't support nonterminal attach. Another thing that needs docs.

>We don't have --sig-proxy implemented yet.

...as I was starting to splutter "then why does it work when I try on the command line", I realized: there's something else going on here with docker-autotest. Thank you, I have a new direction to explore tomorrow.
